suggestions.name: MapFormatter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for formatting and displaying Map structures in various human-readable formats. They share formatting logic, indentation handling, and cycle detection capabilities that are distinct from general map utility operations. Extracting them creates a specialized formatter class with clear responsibility boundaries.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of formatting and displaying Map data structures in human-readable formats. The core functionality includes: printing maps with proper indentation, handling nested maps recursively, detecting circular references, providing debug information, and offering utility operations like map inversion and safe size calculation.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "map formatting and display" domain. They work together to provide comprehensive map visualization capabilities - from simple size checking to complex nested structure printing with cycle detection. The methods share the INDENT_STRING field and work cohesively to handle various aspects of map presentation.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates map formatting/display logic from general map utilities
- Code Reusability: The formatter can be used independently by any component needing map visualization
- Complexity Reduction: Removes complex printing logic from the general MapUtils class
- Testability: Map formatting behavior can be tested in isolation with controlled PrintStream outputs

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate formatting concerns from general utility operations. The extracted class represents a specialized formatter that encapsulates the complex logic of map visualization, cycle detection, and hierarchical printing - a classic example of the Single Responsibility Principle.

suggestions.name: MapPopulator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for populating maps from iterable collections using transformation functions. They share the same algorithmic pattern and domain focus, making them ideal candidates for extraction into a specialized MapPopulator class that encapsulates this specific responsibility.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, focused responsibility: populating maps by transforming elements from an iterable collection. They take source elements, apply transformation functions to generate keys and values, and insert the transformed pairs into target maps. All methods follow the same pattern of iteration, transformation, and population.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods represent the "map population" domain concept - specifically the process of building maps from collections using transformation logic. They handle both regular Map and MultiMap types, with overloaded variants that provide default value transformers. This is a cohesive data transformation subsystem.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates map population logic from other utility functions
- Code Reusability: The population logic can be reused across different contexts beyond MapUtils
- Complexity Reduction: Removes transformation-specific logic from the general-purpose MapUtils class
- Testability: Map population behavior can be tested independently with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate transformation concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion (all perform map population via transformation) and can operate with low coupling (minimal dependencies on external classes beyond the transformation framework).

suggestions.name: MultiValueMapFactory
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive factory unit responsible for creating MultiValueMap instances from various map types and collection configurations. Extracting them into a dedicated factory class separates the specialized creation logic from general map utilities, following the Factory pattern and improving code organization through clear separation of concerns.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, focused responsibility: creating MultiValueMap instances from existing Map objects. All three methods are factory methods that wrap different types of maps into MultiValueMap decorators, with variations for different collection types and factories.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "MultiValueMap creation" domain. They represent different factory methods for instantiating MultiValueMap objects, handling various scenarios like default collections, specific collection classes, and custom collection factories. This is a classic Factory pattern implementation.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates MultiValueMap creation logic from general map utilities
- Enhances Reusability: Factory can be used independently and extended with additional creation methods
- Reduces Complexity: Removes specialized creation logic from the general-purpose MapUtils class
- Better Testability: MultiValueMap creation logic can be tested in isolation

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern combined with the Factory design pattern. These methods represent a cohesive factory responsibility that should be separated from general map utility operations, creating a specialized factory class with high cohesion around MultiValueMap instantiation.

suggestions.name: MapNumericConverter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for converting map values to specific numeric types with null safety and default value handling. Extracting them creates a focused converter that encapsulates all numeric type conversion logic, improving reusability and separating this specialized concern from general map operations.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of extracting and converting values from maps into specific numeric types (Byte, Double, Float, Integer, Long, Number, Short). They handle type conversion, null safety, string parsing, and provide default value mechanisms through functions or direct values.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "type-safe map value extraction" domain. They represent a cohesive subsystem for safely retrieving and converting map values to numeric types, with consistent error handling and default value support. The core concept is transforming loosely-typed map values into strongly-typed numeric objects.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates type conversion logic from general map utilities
- Code reusability: Can be used independently by any class needing safe numeric extraction from maps
- Complexity reduction: Removes 15+ methods from MapUtils, making it more focused
- Testability: Numeric conversion edge cases can be tested in isolation
- Consistency: Centralizes the pattern of null-safe extraction with default value handling

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns. The Converter pattern is appropriate here as these methods transform data from one representation (generic map values) to another (specific numeric types). The class will have high cohesion (all methods perform numeric conversion) and low coupling (minimal dependencies beyond basic Java types).

suggestions.name: MapAccessor
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on safely accessing and retrieving values from Map data structures. They provide null-safe access patterns and format conversion capabilities that represent a single, well-defined responsibility around map data retrieval operations.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share the primary responsibility of safely accessing and retrieving values from Map data structures. The getObject methods provide null-safe map access with optional default values, while toMap converts ResourceBundle objects into Map format. All methods focus on map data retrieval and conversion operations.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods belong to the "map data access" domain. They represent a cohesive subsystem for safely retrieving values from map-like data structures, handling null cases gracefully, and converting between different key-value data formats (ResourceBundle to Map).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates map access logic from other utility operations
- Code Reusability: Map access patterns can be reused across different contexts
- Complexity Reduction: Removes map-specific logic from the larger MapUtils class
- Testability: Map access behavior can be tested independently with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate map access concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion around safe map value retrieval and low coupling with minimal external dependencies. This creates a focused component following the Single Responsibility Principle.
